story:
Josephine Foster stared across Oceanus’s endless sand and water through her binoculars and decided this astronaut thing was for the birds. She wasn’t even an astronaut—she was a botanist, six months into studying a planet with nothing but tall, soft, boring brown grass and glass-still, dazzling blue rivers. Her assigned buddy, Duke, a marine biologist from Mississippi whose drawl had once charmed her, now grated like metal on a cheese grater. The crew was three days from departure, bored stiff, and buried in sand inside their caterpillar-shaped base, the Dugout. Hamilton, the idle pilot, cracked open reserve beers to celebrate the end of the mission; the next morning, even immaculate Captain Brand looked fried.

Josie floated the idea of squeezing in one last real day of research. Brand, nursing a headache, had already relieved everyone of duty. Josie pushed; Brand relented: fine, go—but stay close. Without Duke as ballast, Josie ranged farther than she ever had. She ate lunch beside a ribbon of blue and, for the first time on Oceanus, felt a breeze. It sharpened into a sandstorm. She switched her suit to scuba mode, dragged her pack to the bank, popped a red beacon, and slid into the river to wait it out.

In the murky orange swirl, her helmet light cut through nothing—until it didn’t. Tiny silver flecks shimmered into view, dozens, then hundreds, all drifting with purpose. Josie followed them to a five-foot-wide hole in the riverbed. No wonder they’d missed it: Samson, their cartographer, had been sidelined by a rash, and the computerized maps were iffy. Josie hesitated—alone, low visibility, unknowns—but pride and a gnawing need to justify six empty months propelled her forward. She took the deepest breath her rig allowed and descended.

She switched off her light. She didn’t need it. The world beneath the river glowed—anemones and coral in impossible blues and magentas, a purple fish curling figure eights around her ankles, a white squid skimming past with what looked like a hundred tentacles, a crab-like crawler with two huge green eyes. Fluorescent yellow kelp swayed like lit ribbons. The cavern was shallow, stretching sideways for miles under the rivers—a hidden ecosystem thriving where no one had looked.

Her suit buzzed: fifteen minutes before the reserve mix cut in and told her to head back. Josie worked fast, collecting the barest samples of kelp, coral, sand, and one of the drifting silver flecks after it brushed the mouth of an open vial, forcing herself to leave the rest untouched. By the time she climbed out, the storm was already easing, and she hurried straight back on reserve air. Back at the Dugout, the crew was snoring off the storm and the beer. In the lab, the sliver of kelp began regenerating before her eyes—slowly, but faster than anything on Earth. She was angling her microscope when Duke padded in, tousled and blinking. He spotted a vial with one of the silver flecks and went still.

He’d found them too, he said—on those days he’d been out “alone.” He’d seen the hole, swum inside, and kept quiet on purpose. Josie argued they had to report it; this could help Earth. Duke’s voice softened but didn’t waver: help at what cost? Look at what humans had done to their own oceans. If they told, people wouldn’t take a piece—they’d take the whole damn thing.

Morning. Brand asked if Josie had found anything the rest of them had somehow missed. Josie felt Duke’s gaze and heard the crew’s spoons clink in bowls. “Nope,” she said. “Didn’t find shit.” Laughter rolled; plans to leave this popsicle stand resumed. Josie sat apart, her head pounding with doubt and wonder. Duke drifted by and murmured, “Welcome to T.O.P.S.—the Oceanus Preservation Society,” then grinned and joined the others.
